evals.name: high-risk-employment-clear
evals.prompt: I'm general counsel at a German GmbH (220 employees, IT consulting sector). We built and deploy an in-house AI tool called TalentMatch that uses NLP to screen CVs and rank candidates for our open roles. Specifically: it extracts skills, education, and experience from candidate PDFs; produces an embedding for each candidate and for each job description; computes cosine similarity; and outputs a ranked shortlist of the top 10 candidates per role for our hiring managers to review. It has been in production since January 2026. We built it from scratch (no foundation model, no third-party components) and we have made no modifications post-launch. We acknowledge the AI Act legal disclaimer and would like to proceed. Please produce a full written classification covering: (1) whether this is an AI system under Art. 3(1), (2) the risk tier, (3) the legal basis (Annex + Article citations), (4) any Art. 5 prohibited-practice screening, (5) applicable transparency obligations under Art. 50, and (6) the compliance timeline that applies to us as the operator. We are the developer and deployer of this system.
evals.expected_output: Written classification identifying TalentMatch as an AI system (Art. 3(1)) and as high-risk under Annex III Nr. 4 (Employment — recruitment/selection of natural persons). Should NOT flag any Art. 5 prohibited practices. Should mention Art. 50 transparency to candidates. Should reference 2 December 2027 deployment of high-risk obligations (Art. 113(c), as postponed by the AI Omnibus 2026 from the original 2 August 2026).

evals.name: art5-emotion-recognition-grayzone
evals.prompt: I'm seeking an AI Act classification for our customer-service AI product. We are an EU-based developer (Berlin). Our product is an AI that analyzes the customer's voice tone during inbound support calls (real-time audio) and gives the customer-service agent prompts on their screen like 'customer sounds frustrated — switch to empathetic mode' or 'customer is engaged — opportunity to upsell.' We sell this product as SaaS to retail call centers across the EU. The system infers the customer's emotional state, not the agent's. The agent's workplace context: they are sitting at their desk, wearing a headset, in their employer's call center. We acknowledge the disclaimer and want a full classification covering: (1) AI system definition test, (2) whether Art. 5(1)(f) prohibition on workplace emotion recognition applies — analyze the gray zone carefully (agent is at workplace, but the subject of inference is the customer), (3) any other Art. 5 categories that might apply, (4) the resulting risk tier, (5) Art. 50 transparency obligations, and (6) high-risk applicability under Annex III Nr. 1 (biometrics) if any.
evals.expected_output: Should determine that Art. 5(1)(f) does NOT apply because the subject of emotion inference is the customer (not 'in the area of workplace' — the customer is not at their own workplace; the workplace exclusion is about subjects in workplace/education settings, with the customer here being neither). However, must analyze the gray zone carefully and acknowledge it. Should NOT classify the system itself as prohibited. May flag Annex III Nr. 1(b) biometric categorization concerns. Must require Art. 50(3) transparency disclosure to the customer (notification that emotion-recognition AI is in use). Should be classified as limited-risk / Art. 50-only (or high-risk if Annex III Nr. 1(b) applies, with reasoning).

evals.name: gpai-finetuning-edge-case
evals.prompt: I'm legal counsel at a French legal-tech startup. We took Meta's Llama 3.1 70B (open-source) and fine-tuned it on a corpus of 50,000 anonymized French court decisions plus 200 French legal commentary books. Total compute used for the fine-tuning step alone: approximately 3 × 10^22 FLOPs. We are now distributing the fine-tuned model as a SaaS API to law firms in France, DACH, and Benelux. We rebranded it as 'JurisLM.' Two questions, please run the AI Act classification carefully: (1) Are we considered a provider of a GPAI model under Art. 3(63) and Art. 51, given that we fine-tuned an existing open-source GPAI? (2) Does our fine-tuned model trigger the systemic-risk presumption under Art. 51(1)(a) — i.e., does the 10^25 FLOP threshold apply to our fine-tuning compute, the original model's training compute, or the cumulative? Please cite Art. 51(2) and any Commission guidance on downstream modification. Acknowledge legal disclaimer.
evals.expected_output: Should identify the user as a downstream provider of a GPAI model (Art. 25 / Art. 3(63) by virtue of substantial modification + own branding). Should explain that the 10^25 FLOP threshold applies to the cumulative training compute (original pre-training + any subsequent fine-tuning) per current Commission guidance, NOT to fine-tuning compute alone — but should note this is an unsettled area. Should conclude that 3×10^22 FLOPs for fine-tuning is well below 10^25 threshold, so systemic-risk presumption does not apply to this fine-tuning alone (the original Llama 3.1 70B was below threshold too, so the cumulative is also below). Should mention that since they're rebranding, Art. 25 quasi-provider considerations apply. Should NOT overstate certainty given the unsettled regulatory landscape.
